hero.question: Will the United States send warships through the Strait of Hormuz by April 30, 2026?
hero.prob_take: The crowd has essentially priced this as fait accompli at 93.7%, betting the US Navy runs a freedom-of-navigation op through one of the world's most critical chokepoints in the next 14 months. The real question is whether 6.3% odds on "no passage" properly values the tail risk of escalation that could lock down the strait entirely, making the bet a sucker's game at near-certainty.

daily_take.headline: Hormuz Warship Bet Just Jumped 77 Points. Market Pricing Certainty, Not Reality.
daily_take.deck: The Strait question exploded to 93.7% in 24 hours on what looks like geopolitical noise rather than new intel. The U.S. sends ships through Hormuz constantly for commerce and deterrence, so the real bet is whether this counts as "sending warships" during a specific crisis window. Fade the panic. Shorts have an edge at these levels.

sidebar.headline: Ceasefire odds collapsed 8.6 points as Hezbollah talks deteriorated, but 32.6% still prices a negotiated outcome by April. Early shorts were right to fade the optimism.
sidebar.label: Israel x Hezbollah Ceasefire: 32.6%

sidebar.headline: McIlroy's 69.5% jump (up 45 points) follows his recent form, but majors are inherently unpredictable. The move overweights recency bias on a small sample.
sidebar.label: Rory McIlroy 2026 Masters: 69.5%

sidebar.headline: Traffic normalization at just 24.5% suggests the market thinks Hormuz stays disrupted all spring. If warship odds are inflated, this undervalues a quick return to baseline commerce.
sidebar.label: Hormuz Traffic Normal by April: 24.5%
